data.title: Is It Too Late To Reassess Grainger (GWW) After Its Strong Multi Year Share Price Run?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether W.W. Grainger's current share price reflects its underlying value, you are not alone in asking what you are really paying for at today's levels.
  • The stock last closed at US$1,172.07, with returns of 4.9% over 7 days, 5.5% over 30 days, 16.8% year to date, 19.0% over 1 year, 84.8% over 3 years and 203.7% over 5 years.
  • Recent attention on W.W. Grainger has focused on its role as a major distributor in the capital goods space and how that position fits into long term industrial demand. Investors have been weighing this backdrop as they assess whether the recent share price performance is supported by fundamentals or implies a richer valuation.
  • Despite the strong share price history, W.W. Grainger currently scores 0 out of 6 on Simply Wall St's valuation checks. The next sections will compare different valuation approaches and then conclude with a broader framework that can help you judge value more confidently.

W.W. Grainger scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: W.W. Grainger Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It focuses on the cash the business could return to shareholders rather than short term earnings swings.

For W.W. Grainger, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $1.43b. Analysts provide explicit Free Cash Flow estimates out to 2030, for example $1.62b in 2026 and $2.36b in 2030. Later years are extrapolated by Simply Wall St based on these inputs.

When all projected cash flows are discounted back to today and summed, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $953.97 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $1,172.07, this DCF view implies the stock is around 22.9% overvalued.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests W.W. Grainger may be overvalued by 22.9%. Discover 58 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for W.W. Grainger.

Approach 2: W.W. Grainger Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like W.W. Grainger, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay per share to the earnings that each share generates. Investors typically accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger earnings growth or see the business as lower risk, and a lower P/E when growth expectations are more modest or risks are higher.

W.W. Grainger currently trades on a P/E of 32.52x. This sits above the Trade Distributors industry average P/E of 22.87x and above the peer average of 26.04x. This means the market is currently assigning a richer earnings multiple than these broader benchmarks.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for W.W. Grainger is 28.99x. This is a proprietary estimate of what a reasonable P/E could be for the company, using factors such as its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market capitalisation and risk. This makes it more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the industry, which treat all companies in the group as if they deserved the same multiple.

Comparing the Fair Ratio of 28.99x with the actual P/E of 32.52x suggests W.W. Grainger is trading on a richer multiple than this fair value estimate.

Result: OVERVALUED

data.title: Investors House Oyj (HEL:INVEST) Will Pay A €0.25 Dividend In Three Days
data.description:
Investors House Oyj (HEL:INVEST) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next three days. Typically, the ex-dividend date is two business days before the record date, which is the date on which a company determines the shareholders eligible to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is important because any transaction on a stock needs to have been settled before the record date in order to be eligible for a dividend. Therefore, if you purchase Investors House Oyj's shares on or after the 15th of April, you won't be eligible to receive the dividend, when it is paid on the 23rd of April.

The company's next dividend payment will be €0.25 per share. Last year, in total, the company distributed €0.37 to shareholders. Looking at the last 12 months of distributions, Investors House Oyj has a trailing yield of approximately 8.4% on its current stock price of €4.40. We love seeing companies pay a dividend, but it's also important to be sure that laying the golden eggs isn't going to kill our golden goose! So we need to check whether the dividend payments are covered, and if earnings are growing.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

If a company pays out more in dividends than it earned, then the dividend might become unsustainable - hardly an ideal situation. Investors House Oyj paid out 54% of its earnings to investors last year, a normal payout level for most businesses. A useful secondary check can be to evaluate whether Investors House Oyj generated enough free cash flow to afford its dividend. It paid out an unsustainably high 295% of its free cash flow as dividends over the past 12 months, which is worrying. It's pretty hard to pay out more than you earn, so we wonder how Investors House Oyj intends to continue funding this dividend, or if it could be forced to cut the payment.

While Investors House Oyj's dividends were covered by the company's reported profits, cash is somewhat more important, so it's not great to see that the company didn't generate enough cash to pay its dividend. Cash is king, as they say, and were Investors House Oyj to repeatedly pay dividends that aren't well covered by cashflow, we would consider this a warning sign.

Check out our latest analysis for Investors House Oyj

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Companies with consistently growing earnings per share generally make the best dividend stocks, as they usually find it easier to grow dividends per share. If business enters a downturn and the dividend is cut, the company could see its value fall precipitously. That's why it's comforting to see Investors House Oyj's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 49% per annum for the past five years. Earnings have been growing quickly, but we're concerned dividend payments consumed most of the company's cash flow over the past year.

Many investors will assess a company's dividend performance by evaluating how much the dividend payments have changed over time. Investors House Oyj has delivered 8.1% dividend growth per year on average over the past 10 years. We're glad to see dividends rising alongside earnings over a number of years, which may be a sign the company intends to share the growth with shareholders.

Final Takeaway

Has Investors House Oyj got what it takes to maintain its dividend payments? The best dividend stocks typically boast a long history of growing earnings per share (EPS) via a combination of earnings growth and buybacks. So, you might think that Investors House Oyj buying back stock, growing its EPS, and retaining profits within its business is a good combination. However, we note with some concern that it paid out 295% of its free cash flow last year, which is uncomfortably high and makes us wonder why the company chose to spend even more cash on buybacks. Overall, it's not a bad combination, but we feel that there are likely more attractive dividend prospects out there.

With that being said, if dividends aren't your biggest concern with Investors House Oyj, you should know about the other risks facing this business. Our analysis shows 4 warning signs for Investors House Oyj that we strongly recommend you have a look at before investing in the company.

If you're in the market for strong dividend payers, we recommend checking our selection of top dividend stocks.

data.title: FedEx Pilot Deal And Freight Spinoff Reframe Costs And Growth Targets
data.description:
• FedEx (NYSE:FDX) has reached a tentative 5 year labor agreement with its pilots' union after almost five years of negotiations.
  • The proposed contract covers more than 5,000 pilots and includes changes to compensation and working conditions.
  • This agreement comes as FedEx Freight prepares for a planned spinoff into an independent business.
  • Management has also outlined medium term growth targets and operational priorities across FedEx's air and ground networks.

FedEx operates a global package delivery and logistics network that spans express air services, ground operations, and freight. The tentative pilot agreement lands at a time when parcel and freight operators are reassessing cost structures, service levels, and capital allocation. For readers tracking NYSE:FDX, these parallel moves tie directly into how the company organizes and prices its core services.

Looking ahead, investors will be watching whether pilots ratify the tentative contract, how FedEx executes the Freight spinoff, and how management aligns its medium term goals with day to day operations. The combination of labor terms, network design, and separate freight ownership could play an important role in how FedEx balances reliability, flexibility, and cost discipline over the coming years.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for FedEx by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on FedEx.

We've flagged 1 risk for FedEx. See which could impact your investment.

The tentative 5 year pilot contract and the planned FedEx Freight spinoff sit at the center of how FedEx reshapes its business model. A roughly 40% step up in pilot pay and retroactive compensation increases fixed costs in the air network, but it may also reduce the risk of work disruptions and help with retention after a period of historic resignations. At the same time, Network 2.0 and DRIVE are closing facilities and redesigning routes to lower overall unit costs, so the key question is whether structural savings and better aircraft utilization offset the richer labor terms.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The FedEx Narrative

  • The tentative deal supports the narrative that cost programs like DRIVE and Network 2.0 can work alongside a more secure labor footing, which may help FedEx run its global air network more predictably.
  • Higher pilot wages could challenge margin goals if freight volumes soften or pricing power weakens, especially as FedEx Freight separates and can no longer be managed inside one integrated profit pool.
  • The Freight spinoff adds an independent less than truckload operator with its own management team and targets, an element that long term narratives may not fully reflect in terms of stand alone execution risk and capital allocation choices.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for FedEx to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Higher long term pilot compensation could pressure FedEx Express margins if cost savings from Network 2.0 and DRIVE, or pricing actions versus UPS and DHL, do not keep pace.
  • ⚠️ The Freight spinoff introduces separation and stand alone execution risk, including the possibility that synergies with the broader FedEx network are harder to preserve than planned.
  • 🎁 Labor stability at the airline, if the deal is ratified, may support more reliable service levels for express customers and reduce the risk of disruptions relative to competitors.
  • 🎁 A focused FedEx Freight business, with its own targets for revenue growth and operating income, could sharpen commercial efforts in less than truckload and improve how capital is allocated between air and ground.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on three things. First, whether pilots ratify the agreement and how FedEx discloses the contract’s impact on unit costs in its air network. Second, the detail that emerges from Freight investor events on pricing, target end markets, and network efficiency once it trades separately from FedEx. Third, how management updates DRIVE and Network 2.0 milestones as facilities close and volumes shift across air and ground, particularly in comparison with peers such as UPS and DHL in parcels and Old Dominion in less than truckload.

Approach 1: Subsea 7 Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s value. For Subsea 7, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flows available to shareholders.

The latest twelve month free cash flow is about US$1.04b. Analysts provide estimates for several years, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates further to build a 10 year path. In this case, projected free cash flow for 2028 is US$728.47m, with intermediate years such as 2026 and 2027 forecast at US$650.16m and US$743.89m respectively, before extending out to 2035 using modelled assumptions.

When all those cash flows are discounted back, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of NOK 562.92 per share. Against the recent share price of NOK 311.40, this implies the stock is around 44.7% below that intrinsic estimate, which points to the shares trading at a substantial discount.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Subsea 7 Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay for a share to the earnings that company is currently generating. It helps you see how many years of earnings the market is effectively pricing in.

What counts as a "normal" P/E often reflects growth expectations and risk. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually ties to a lower multiple.

Subsea 7 currently trades on a P/E of 23.56x. That is above the Energy Services industry average of 9.01x, and below the peer group average of 40.51x, so the stock sits between those two benchmarks.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Subsea 7 is 12.46x. It can be more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry because it tries to match the multiple to the company’s own profile.
